narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 75% chance that you would not pass— a threshold that leads to rejection to ensure we admit students with the strongest projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your position in Year 3, where you ranked in the second decile among your peers, well below the fifth or higher decile typical for those who passed the bar. That early-year performance in the third decile also played a significant role, as it similarly lagged behind the stronger showings—around the sixth decile on average—of successful bar takers. These two factors were the most influential in pushing the model toward predicting failure, signaling to it a pattern of underperformance relative to proven passers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 38 stood out positively, above the average of about 37 for those who passed, and it noticeably pulled the prediction in the direction of success. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 fell short of the 3.24 average among bar passers, and your lower-middle family income bracket is less common among them (where middle and upper-middle incomes dominate), those weren't the top drivers here. Your choice to pursue full-time studies aligns well with the vast majority of successful students, but it couldn't outweigh the key academic concerns. Compared to typical accepted applicants, who generally rank higher in school deciles and show more consistent academic strength, your profile highlighted risks that the model deemed too high. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps targeting LSAT-level excellence in other areas, and reapply once you've strengthened those foundational metrics. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded on later attempts. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
